city.name: Madrid
city.description: The Madrid Freedom Hunt traces Spain's extraordinary and painful path from Francisco Franco's dictatorship to one of Europe's most vibrant democracies. From Guernica at the Reina Sofía to Goya's Disasters of War at the Prado, from the working-class memory of Lavapiés to the post-Franco explosion of La Movida in Malasaña — each stop connects Spain's democratic struggle to the ongoing fight for American democratic values abroad.
city.suggestedRoute: Parque del Oeste (Templo de Debod) → Malasaña (Dos de Mayo) → Gran Vía (Telefónica) → Centro (Sol/Plaza Mayor/Almudena) → Embajadores (Lavapiés) → Atocha (Reina Sofía/Estación) → Retiro (Prado/Parque) → Centro (Ateneo) → Salesas (Tribunal) → Recoletos (Biblioteca Nacional/Casa de América) (~8.5km)

locations.name: Puerta del Sol
locations.clue: Kilometer Zero of Spain begins here — the literal center of the country's road network. In 1931, the Second Republic was proclaimed from the balcony of the Interior Ministry on this square. In 2011, a generation that had never known Franco took this square and refused to leave. Democracy is always unfinished. Stand in the center and count the radii.
locations.historicalHook: Puerta del Sol has been Madrid's central plaza since the 16th century. Napoleon's troops massacred Spanish civilians here on May 2, 1808 — the scene Goya immortalized. The Second Republic was proclaimed from its balcony in 1931. In May 2011, the 15-M Indignados movement occupied the square for weeks, demanding real democracy beyond the ballot box.
locations.americanConnection: The 2011 Indignados movement — 'We are the 99%' — directly inspired Occupy Wall Street, which launched in New York four months later. Spanish and American protest traditions have fed each other for over a century: American anarchist and socialist movements had deep Spanish roots; the International Brigades were disproportionately American volunteers fighting fascism in Spain years before Pearl Harbor.
locations.challenge: Find the bronze bear and the madroño tree sculpture — Madrid's symbol, standing since 1967. The bear reaches for berries on a tree that has been here for centuries. Then find Kilometer Zero embedded in the pavement beneath the clock tower. Every road in Spain begins at this point.
locations.votingBridge: From this square, the democratic pulse of Spain has been taken for 200 years. Your democratic pulse is measured in votes. Register at votefromabroad.org.

locations.name: Plaza Mayor
locations.clue: Inquisition trials. Royal proclamations. Bullfights. Public executions. Now tourists eating paella under uniform ochre facades — Franco's vision of what Spain should look like: ordered, traditional, without contradiction. But the square remembers everything. It always does. What has your public square witnessed that isn't on the tourist map?
locations.historicalHook: Plaza Mayor was built in the early 17th century as the ceremonial center of imperial Spain. Under the Inquisition, autos-da-fé — public trials and punishments of heretics — were staged here before crowds of thousands. Under Franco, the square was standardized in the 1950s as a symbol of Spanish unity: the uniform ochre paint was applied over the plaza's varied historical colors.
locations.americanConnection: The Spanish Inquisition's tactics — public humiliation, forced confession, guilt by association — have been a template authoritarian governments return to throughout history. McCarthyism's public loyalty hearings before the House Un-American Activities Committee functioned on the same logic: the performance of accusation as verdict. The chamber changed; the furniture was different. The logic was identical.
locations.challenge: Find the Equestrian Statue of Philip III at the plaza's center. He was King of Spain when the Plymouth Colony was founded in 1620. Count how many different national flags you can spot in the crowd — the world fills this square that once tried to define itself against the world.
locations.votingBridge: Public squares are where democracies perform themselves. Your vote is how you participate from wherever you are. Register at votefromabroad.org.

locations.name: Museo Reina Sofía — Guernica
locations.clue: On April 26, 1937, Nazi Germany's Condor Legion bombed the Basque market town of Guernica for three hours — a test of new aerial bombing techniques on a civilian population. Picasso was in Paris. He heard the reports and painted for five weeks straight. The result is 11 feet tall, 25 feet wide, gray as ash, and screaming. It cannot be reproduced. You have to stand in front of it.
locations.historicalHook: Picasso completed Guernica in June 1937 and gave it to the Spanish Republic with one condition: it may not return to Spain until democracy does. It stayed at New York's Museum of Modern Art for 42 years. After Franco died and the Constitution was ratified, it returned to Madrid in 1981, under armed guard. Picasso himself died in 1973, four years before he could have seen Spain free again.
locations.americanConnection: Guernica spent its exile at the Museum of Modern Art in New York — where American audiences stood before it and understood what modern war meant to civilians. Picasso was a Communist, which made him suspect to the US government; he was refused an American visa in 1952. The painting's American home was itself a political act: the world's most powerful democracy sheltering art that couldn't go home.
locations.challenge: Stand in front of Guernica without your phone for at least two minutes. Then identify: the bull, the horse, the mother holding her dead child, the screaming soldier, the lamp. Each figure has been interpreted differently for 85 years. The painting does not resolve. That is not an accident.
locations.votingBridge: Guernica exists because an artist was free to tell the truth about what power does. That freedom requires political protection. Protect it with your vote at votefromabroad.org.

locations.name: Atocha Station Memorial
locations.clue: March 11, 2004: 191 people killed, 2,000 wounded in coordinated train bombings. Three days later, Spanish voters went to the polls in a scheduled election and voted the incumbent government out of power — partly for lying about who was responsible in the hours after the attack. The democratic system worked exactly as it was supposed to. Find the memorial and read the names.
locations.historicalHook: The Madrid train bombings of March 11, 2004 — '11-M' — were the deadliest terrorist attack in Spanish history. The ruling party initially blamed ETA (the Basque separatist organization) and suppressed evidence of Al-Qaeda involvement before the election. Spanish voters, furious at the deception, voted the government out three days after the bombing. The unexpected result stunned European political observers.
locations.americanConnection: The US experienced its own post-terror democratic test after 9/11 — with different results. The Patriot Act passed in 45 days; civil liberties were curtailed; the WMD case went unchallenged until the war was already lost. Spanish voters in 2004 demonstrated that democracies can hold governments accountable even in the immediate aftermath of mass violence. The comparison is instructive.
locations.challenge: Find the cylindrical glass memorial inside Atocha station — a tall transparent tower filled with messages and condolences from victims' families and people across Spain. Read as many messages as you can. Note the names of victims from other countries listed at the base.
locations.votingBridge: Spanish voters held their government accountable three days after a massacre. You can hold yours accountable every election. Register to vote at votefromabroad.org.

locations.name: El Retiro Park — Ángel Caído
locations.clue: This is reputedly the only public monument in the world to the Devil — built in 1878, long before Franco's Spain would have permitted anything so subversive. The Fallen Angel stands in a public park that has been a royal garden, a Republican commons, and a Franco-era nationalist space. Madrid's oldest rose garden blooms here in May. What does a democracy do with a garden built by kings?
locations.historicalHook: El Retiro was the royal garden of the Spanish monarchy until 1869, when the democratic revolutionary government opened it to the public. The Ángel Caído (Fallen Angel) sculpture installed in 1878 is the only public statue in the world depicting Lucifer's fall — unusual for a staunchly Catholic country. During the Civil War, the park hosted Republican anti-aircraft batteries; under Franco, it was reclaimed for patriotic ceremonies.
locations.americanConnection: American public parks — built during the Progressive Era as democratic spaces where class distinctions dissolved — share the same philosophy as Retiro: a space returned from royal privilege to public use. The tension between public space as democratic commons and as spectacle for power plays out in American cities constantly, from debates over Confederate monuments to how parks were policed during the 2020 protests.
locations.challenge: Find the Ángel Caído statue and note its location — city engineers claim it sits exactly 666 meters above sea level, a placement they say was intentional. Then find the nearby Palacio de Cristal: a greenhouse built in 1887, now an art exhibition space. Photograph the iron-and-glass walls reflecting the trees.
locations.votingBridge: Public spaces are political choices. Who your parks serve — and how they are governed — is determined by elections. Vote.

locations.name: Almudena Cathedral & Royal Palace
locations.clue: Franco needed the Catholic Church and the Church needed Franco. The Spanish Church endorsed the 1936 coup, called it a crusade, and received state funding, control of education, and legal privilege for generations. The Almudena Cathedral took 110 years to build — begun in 1879, consecrated in 1993, by which point the country it was built for had changed beyond recognition. Power takes a long time to build. It can fall faster.
locations.historicalHook: Franco's regime was explicitly built on 'National Catholicism' — the fusion of Spanish identity with Catholic orthodoxy. The 1953 Concordat with the Vatican gave the Church state funding, educational control, and tax exemptions. Priests blessed the executions of Republican prisoners. The alliance began unraveling only when Vatican II liberalized Catholic doctrine in the 1960s, producing a generation of reformist Spanish clergy who eventually opposed the regime.
locations.americanConnection: The American constitutional separation of church and state has been contested since the founding. Contemporary Christian nationalism argues for the same fusion of national identity and religious orthodoxy that Franco institutionalized. Spanish history offers a 40-year data set on what that looks like in practice: a Catholic state that outlawed divorce, banned contraception, criminalized homosexuality, and controlled what children were permitted to learn.
locations.challenge: Find the coat of arms of Madrid above the Almudena's neogothic entrance. Then cross to the Plaza de la Armería in front of the Royal Palace — the space where Franco held victory parades after the Civil War. Photograph the Palace's facade. The view from this plaza was designed to project power across the city.
locations.votingBridge: The separation of church and state is a live issue in every election. Your vote speaks to this fundamental democratic principle. Register at votefromabroad.org.

locations.name: Lavapiés — El Rastro Market
locations.clue: El Rastro has been an open-air market in Lavapiés on Sunday mornings since at least the 18th century. Lavapiés has always been Madrid's working-class, unrespectable neighborhood — Jewish in the medieval period, Romani, Moroccan, Bangladeshi, and now rapidly gentrifying. It was a Republican stronghold. The people who have lived here across the centuries share one thing: they were not who power was designed for.
locations.historicalHook: Lavapiés was Madrid's Jewish quarter before the Inquisition, then a working-class district that was one of the most politically radical parts of the city during the Republic. During the Civil War, it was a center of anarchist organizing. Under Franco, its residents bore the worst of economic privation and political repression. Today it is one of the most diverse neighborhoods in Spain — and one of the most rapidly gentrifying.
locations.americanConnection: American cities have their own Lavapiés: neighborhoods that absorbed each wave of immigration, labor radicalism, and working-class life — and are now being displaced by luxury development. The political history of these places — from Chicago's Back of the Yards to Harlem to the Mission District — is the history of American labor organizing, civil rights, and progressive politics. They share a consistent trajectory.
locations.challenge: Walk through El Rastro (Sunday mornings) or along Ribera de Curtidores any day and find a vendor selling something that surprises you. Note the diversity of people working and shopping here — this market has operated continuously through the Inquisition, the Bourbon monarchy, two Republics, a dictatorship, and democracy.
locations.votingBridge: Immigration policy, labor rights, and housing affordability are all ballot issues. Every election shapes neighborhoods like this one. Register to vote at votefromabroad.org.

locations.name: Plaza del Dos de Mayo — Malasaña
locations.clue: Franco died November 20, 1975. By 1976, Malasaña was already changing. By 1980, it was the center of La Movida Madrileña — a wild, queer, punk, cinematic explosion of freedom after 40 years of repression. Almodóvar filmed here. Bands played all night. People were openly gay in public for the first time in their lives. The plaza named for Napoleon's defeat became the heart of Spain's liberation from itself.
locations.historicalHook: La Movida Madrileña (1975-1985) was a cultural explosion that erupted immediately after Franco's death. Censorship ended; LGBTQ+ life became visible; art, film, fashion, and music exploded. Pedro Almodóvar's early films — shot cheaply on the streets of Malasaña — became internationally acclaimed. The movement was Madrid's collective exhale after four decades of enforced silence. Malasaña's Plaza del Dos de Mayo was its symbolic center.
locations.americanConnection: The AIDS crisis devastated Madrid's La Movida community in the mid-1980s, simultaneously with New York's and San Francisco's gay communities. The parallel timelines — liberation followed by catastrophe — produced the same political response: ACT UP in America, the Socialist PSOE government's eventual public health response in Spain. Art made under censorship and art made under plague have sustained each other across national borders.
locations.challenge: Find the Arco de Monteleón in the plaza — the arch from the cavalry barracks that stood here during Napoleon's 1808 attack, all that remains of that battle. Read the commemorative plaques. Then find a bar with photographs or posters from the La Movida era — Malasaña is full of them.
locations.votingBridge: The freedom of La Movida was protected by a democratic government elected in 1982. LGBTQ+ rights, artistic freedom, and public health policy are still determined by elections. Vote.

locations.name: Telefónica Building — Gran Vía
locations.theme: The War Comes to the City
locations.clue: The Telefónica skyscraper, built in 1929, was one of the first modern skyscrapers in Europe. During the Spanish Civil War it became the most important press tower in the world. Hemingway filed dispatches from here. Robert Capa photographed from here. Franco's artillery targeted the building 118 times. It still stands. The journalists who worked here were writing the first draft of history for a world that mostly looked away. Are you paying attention?
locations.historicalHook: The Telefónica Building was used as the Republican government's communications hub during the Siege of Madrid (1936-1939). International correspondents — including Ernest Hemingway, Martha Gellhorn, and John Dos Passos — worked from its upper floors while Franco's forces shelled it repeatedly. Madrid held out against Franco for nearly three years — far longer than anyone expected when the siege began.
locations.americanConnection: Hemingway's For Whom the Bell Tolls (1940) made the Spanish Civil War legible to American readers. Robert Capa's photographs from this building established the visual language of modern war photography. American volunteers — the Abraham Lincoln Brigade — fought for the Republic; they were later investigated by HUAC as 'premature antifascists.' The US government's official neutrality during the Spanish Civil War is widely considered a moral failure of the Roosevelt administration.
locations.challenge: Look up at the Telefónica building's facade — find evidence of wartime patching: irregular stonework, slight color variations where repairs were made. Then find the plaque commemorating the journalists who worked here. The building survived 118 artillery strikes. Count the patches you can see.
locations.votingBridge: Press freedom and the ability of journalists to report on power is shaped by who governs. Your vote shapes that environment. Register at votefromabroad.org.

locations.name: Museo del Prado — Goya's War Rooms
locations.clue: Francisco de Goya survived the Napoleonic invasion, painted it, and went deaf. He survived the Inquisition, painted it, and withdrew into himself. In his last years, he covered his dining room walls with black paintings of monsters, cannibals, and Saturn devouring his son. He never titled them. We did. Find the Disasters of War prints. He made them to testify. No one could publish them while he was alive.
locations.historicalHook: Goya (1746-1828) served as court painter to the Spanish royal family while secretly documenting the horrors of the Napoleonic invasion in his Disasters of War series — 82 prints showing atrocities committed by both sides, not published until 1863. His Black Paintings, applied directly to the walls of his house, include Saturn Devouring His Son — widely interpreted as the state consuming its own people.
locations.americanConnection: Goya's Disasters of War established a tradition of art as bearing witness that runs through American war photography — from Mathew Brady's Civil War images to Nick Ut's 'Napalm Girl.' The question he raised — what is the obligation of the artist who sees what power does? — is live in every American newspaper, documentary film, and social media platform that operates under political pressure today.
locations.challenge: Find Saturn Devouring His Son in the Black Paintings room. Stand in front of it for sixty seconds — look at the knuckles, the eyes, the half-eaten body. Then find the Disasters of War prints in the collection. Read the captions Goya himself wrote on each one. They are as important as the images.
locations.votingBridge: What Goya painted in secret, artists today photograph and publish — when they are free to. A free press requires a democracy that protects it. Vote to protect it at votefromabroad.org.

locations.name: Ateneo de Madrid
locations.clue: Founded in 1835 as a private club for liberal intellectuals in a deeply conservative country, the Ateneo survived the Inquisition's legacy, the restoration monarchy, the Civil War, and Franco — though Franco stripped it of independence and placed it under state control. Spain's great republicans, writers, scientists, and reformers met here. It still has a library and a lecture hall. Some fights go on very quietly for a very long time.
locations.historicalHook: The Ateneo de Madrid has been the intellectual heart of Spanish liberal culture since 1835. Its members have included virtually every major Spanish intellectual, scientist, and political figure: Pérez Galdós, Unamuno, Antonio Machado, and Ortega y Gasset all lectured here. During the Republic, it was a center of democratic thought. Under Franco, it was nationalized and its independence curtailed. It reopened freely after the transition.
locations.americanConnection: American intellectual life has institutional counterparts: the ACLU (founded 1920), university free-speech traditions, independent journals, and think tanks. Like the Ateneo, these are the infrastructure of democratic culture — places where ideas hostile to existing power are permitted to develop. They are consistently among the first targets when democracies begin to erode, defunded, delegitimized, or brought under political control.
locations.challenge: Find the Ateneo on Calle del Prado. If it is open, enter the library reading room — one of the finest in Madrid. If not, find the facade and read the names of past presidents listed there. Identify which ones went on to lead the Spanish Republic and which were subsequently imprisoned or exiled by Franco.
locations.votingBridge: Democratic culture is built and maintained by institutions. Funding those institutions — universities, libraries, public media — is a political choice made in every election. Register at votefromabroad.org.

locations.name: Tribunal Supremo
locations.clue: Spain's Supreme Court sits in a former convent. In 1998, a judge named Baltasar Garzón used universal jurisdiction to indict Augusto Pinochet for crimes against humanity — in a Spanish court, for crimes committed in Chile. He was later disbarred for trying to investigate Franco's own crimes. Justice doesn't flow evenly. It requires people willing to pursue it and institutions willing to protect them.
locations.historicalHook: Spain's democratic transition was negotiated through the 'Pact of Forgetting' — an amnesty law passed in 1977 that protected all actors from the Civil War and dictatorship from prosecution. This enabled the transition but denied justice to victims. Judge Baltasar Garzón spent his career challenging this impunity, prosecuting Pinochet internationally and attempting to investigate Franco-era crimes, before being disbarred by the Supreme Court in 2012.
locations.americanConnection: American transitional justice is equally incomplete: the accommodation of post-WWII German scientists under Operation Paperclip, the non-prosecution of CIA torture architects after Abu Ghraib, the pardon of Nixon. The question of how much accountability a democracy requires to heal has no clean answer — only political choices about how much impunity is acceptable. Those choices are made by elected officials.
locations.challenge: Find the Tribunal Supremo facade on Plaza de la Villa de París. Look for the inscription above the entrance and the allegorical sculptures representing Justice and Law. Photograph the scales above the door. Then find the nearby Las Salesas church — the former royal convent that became Spain's first Supreme Court in 1872.
locations.votingBridge: Judicial independence is protected — or undermined — by governments chosen by voters. Your vote reaches the courts. Register at votefromabroad.org.

locations.name: Biblioteca Nacional de España
locations.clue: Franco's censorship apparatus reviewed every book, film, song, and radio program from 1938 to 1977. Authors submitted manuscripts; censors returned them with pages marked for deletion. The National Library holds copies of thousands of works banned under Franco — books that were legal everywhere else in the world. A library is a collective memory. Someone has to defend it.
locations.historicalHook: The Franco regime's censorship apparatus reviewed all print, broadcast, and film content. Books by Republicans, liberals, Communists, and 'immoral' authors were banned. Foreign books required special clearance. Spanish authors learned to write in code — ellipsis, allegory, surrealism — to survive. The Biblioteca Nacional, founded in 1712, held censored works in restricted collections accessible only to approved researchers.
locations.americanConnection: The American Library Association documents book banning in US schools and libraries. In 2022-2023, more books were challenged in American public libraries than in any year since tracking began. Many targeted books concern LGBTQ+ people, racial justice, and the Holocaust. The throughline from Franco's censors to contemporary American school boards is a consistent conviction that some people's stories should not be read by children.
locations.challenge: Find the Biblioteca Nacional's main facade on Paseo de los Recoletos. Look for the allegorical sculptures at the entrance — they represent Science and the Arts. Find the inscription above the door. Then find the Museo Arqueológico Nacional attached to the same building — both institutions share one neoclassical monument to knowledge. Photograph the combined facade.
locations.votingBridge: Book banning in American schools is determined by school board elections and state legislatures. Those are on your ballot too. Register at votefromabroad.org.

locations.name: Templo de Debod — Parque del Oeste
locations.theme: The Front Line at the University
locations.clue: An Egyptian temple gifted to Spain in 1968 — relocated from the Nile because the Aswan Dam would have flooded it — stands in a park that looks peaceful until you know that 200 meters north is the Ciudad Universitaria, where the Civil War front line was fixed for three years. Where students and professors fought in the trenches they dug between lecture halls. Where library books were used to build sandbags.
locations.historicalHook: The Ciudad Universitaria (University City) in Madrid was the front line of the Siege of Madrid from late 1936 to 1939 — one of the longest urban sieges in modern warfare. Franco's forces advanced to the university but were held there by Republican defenders, including the International Brigades, for over three years. The university buildings were used as defensive positions; libraries were gutted; professors both taught and died in the trenches between their own classrooms.
locations.americanConnection: American members of the Abraham Lincoln Brigade — an International Brigades unit — fought in the trenches of Ciudad Universitaria. They were the first Americans to fight fascism, years before Pearl Harbor. When they returned home, many were blacklisted by HUAC as Communists. The Veterans of the Abraham Lincoln Brigade (VALB) fought for recognition for decades, finally honored by Congress in 1996.
locations.challenge: Walk from the Templo de Debod toward the Ciudad Universitaria. Find the commemorative plaque at the edge of Parque del Oeste marking the Civil War battle for the university. Then photograph the Egyptian temple at sunset if possible — ancient Egypt, Spanish Civil War history, and post-Franco memory all occupy this same ground.
locations.votingBridge: Students and professors died defending a university — literally, in the trenches between classrooms. Education and academic freedom are on the ballot in every election. Exercise your vote to protect them at votefromabroad.org.

locations.name: Casa de América — Americans Abroad Registration Hub
locations.theme: FINISH LINE — The American in Madrid
locations.clue: The Palacio de Linares was built in 1873 for a man who made his fortune in colonial Cuba. Now it houses Casa de América — Spain's institution for US-Latin American-Spanish dialogue. You are an American living in Europe. You've walked through the history of fascism and resistance. You've connected Spanish struggle to American democracy. You've earned this: find the voter registration table, complete the form, and become the thing this entire walk has been about.
locations.historicalHook: The Palacio de Linares was completed in 1873 during Spain's brief First Republic — the only republican government Spain would have until 1931. Its builder made his fortune in colonial Cuba. It became Casa de América in 1992, Spain's institution for promoting dialogue between the United States, Spain, and Latin America. Democrats Abroad Spain uses it as a hub for expat civic engagement and voter registration.
locations.americanConnection: You are an American who chose to live in Europe. Every stop on this walk was chosen because the history of European fascism and European democracy is also the history of American democracy — the threats overlap, the lessons transfer, and the stakes are shared. You are not a bystander in American politics because you live abroad. You are exactly the kind of citizen the founding generation imagined: free to go where you choose, obligated to participate in what you've left behind.
locations.challenge: Find the Democrats Abroad Spain registration table inside Casa de América or at the nearby café. Complete your voter registration. If you're already registered, confirm your status at votefromabroad.org. Then share that you completed the Madrid Freedom Hunt with #FreedomHunt.
locations.votingBridge: You did it. Register now at votefromabroad.org. Democrats Abroad Spain can walk you through every step of the process.
